pages_preview.text_preview: DRAFT - Check back frequently for updates! REPORTING MANUAL Instructions for RR labs/teams to follow after finishing data collection for a SCORE replication or reproduction

If you have finished a round of SCORE data collection, this manual is for you. First of all, CONGRATULATIONS! To finish out a project (or determine whether or not to continue to further data collection, if this applies to you), we will be checking over your materials with two goals in mind: (1) Making sure that all SCORE projects have enough of a public scientific record to let them be used by future researchers. (2) Constructing the SCORE ‘meta-dataset’ which documents the outcome of every replication, along with many other variables about the original and replication studies (you can see the equivalent dataset from the Reproducibility Project:Psychology at this link.) This manual walks you through the steps for finishing out your SCORE project and details the requirements we’ll be checking for at each step. A note on public/private repositories: Right now, all parts of your SCORE repository are private. At the end of the project, the expectation is to make everything public that is ethically possible to do so. As you work through this guide and prepare your materials, make sure you are thinking about which pieces of your specific project will be public vs. private. A note for ‘existing data’ replications: This manual was initially designed for projects that involve collecting new datasets, and will be adapted as needed for projects that involve identifying and working with existing data. Please get in touch with Andrew Tyner or Melissa Kline to discuss steps that don’t work well for your project’s workflow! Here is a table of contents for the rest of the manual: Completing your data collection and analysis Your final report Your OSF project Submitting your variables form HRPO - Final steps Funded projects - getting paid! FAQ and Further Resources

Completing your data collection and analysis As you finish data collection and get ready to carry out your analyses, it is a good idea to thoroughly re-read your preregistration, to remind yourselves of the plans you made, and identify places you have already had to diverge from those plans. These divergences are expected, and the most important thing is to report them! You may wish to keep a running list of these changes as you go so you know what happened when. You should aim to upload your materials to your OSF project as you go. Once you finish data collection (including immediately after stage 1), we recommend you finish documenting your work in the following order: (1) Upload your raw data and any finalized versions of stimuli, surveys, or other research materials. (2) Process and analyze your data (specifically, to produce your key H* inferential test), keeping a record (or commented code) that documents how this is done. (3) Upload your analysis code, along with any intermediate (e.g. ‘cleaned’ values, scale calculation/transformations, etc.) versions of your data or any other materials you used to generate your final result. (4) Prepare and upload a final report which includes at least the ‘required’ points discussed below. Once you have completed steps 1-4, you should email the SCORE coordinators (scorecoordinator@cos.io) to let them know you’ve finished data collection. At this point, we will check your project to let you know if you are ready to enter your results into the meta-dataset (and to move on to Stage 2 data collection, if this applies). Here’s what we’ll be checking for:

Your final report Every SCORE project should have a written document that describes the outcome of your replication: This is where a visitor to your OSF project will start to find out what your project is about, what the outcome was, and how they can learn more, access your data, or reproduce your results. You should upload this document as a PDF at the top level of your replication repository, and name it “SCORE Report - YourPaperID.pdf” (e.g. SCORE Report - Smith_JournEco_2012_sc65 - Yourname_32w7.pdf”) You can see some examples of final report documents in the example repository here. Because many of you will be completing your final reports in a short window of time near the end of the project, it is okay for this report to be short (1-2 pages). You may wish to write a briefer version for COS staff to review as we approach final deadlines, and expand to a longer draft later on. Since SCORE projects vary quite a bit, you are free to organize this document how you want. However, you should approach this document as you would a grant proposal - we will be reading large numbers of them in a short period of time, and the easier it is for us to determine whether you’ve met all the requirements, the faster we can free you up to move forward! We recommend you put the required elements right up front, followed by any other information you are including. There are some example sample templates you can use here. Final Report - Required Elements Every project in SCORE needs to have these in the report uploaded to your OSF project - if they are missing, you’ll be asked to revise before moving on. Claim Summary. A summary of the claim you set out to replicate. You will usually find this as the SCORE-provided text from Question 3 of your preregistration. Please do not include the statistical result from the original study (generally the last sentence) in order to prevent any confusion that it is the result of your replication. Example claim Summary: This replication tests the claim from Smith et al. (2004), Experiment 2 that performance in math improves relative to physics during the college

years. The claim was tested using a mixed two-way ANOVA on test scores with independent variables for Year (Freshman, Senior) and Subject (math, physics), where the critical test is the Year x Subject interaction. Replication Criteria. This is usually your key ‘H*’ hypothesis (from Question 4 or 21 of your preregistration). It should clearly state what pattern of results (not just which significant test) would qualify as a replication. Example Replication Criteria (H*): A successful replication of the key SCORE test (H*) would involve a significant Subject x Year interaction such that senior students perform better on math than physics, while Freshman math scores are either equal to or lower than physics scores. Replication Result. A statement of the statistical result of your key test, referencing the inference criteria for your H* in Question 21 of the preregistration. This should be in somewhat greater detail/precision than you might include in a typical paper’s results section, including for instance the exact analytic sample size, effect size, exact p value, etc. This section should also include an explicit statement on whether or not the result replicated according to the SCORE replication criteria! Example Replication Result: The replication included 60 participants in the analytic sample to reach the requirements for stage 1 data collection (n = 55). We found a significant interaction of Subject x Year on test scores (F(1, 56) = 3.41, p = 0.231, two-tailed, uncorrected, partial eta squared = 0.14), such that Freshmen participants’ test scores were roughly equal for math and physics, while Senior students’ math scores were higher than physics scores. Thus, this replication of the claim was successful according to the SCORE criteria. Reporting an exact p-value is a non-negotiable requirement from our funder, please do not report your p-value as a threshold for the focal test. This applies to all results, regardless of whether it is significant or non-significant. If your p-value is so small that it cannot be accurately calculated/reported by your stats software, please let us know. If your analysis is any kind of regression, please report a full table of your model output, including the intercept, non-significant coefficients, all covariates/controls, etc. Please make it clear, in-text and/or within the table itself, which specific coefficient is the “focal” coefficient for SCORE purposes. Make sure to explicitly state what the preregistered analytic sample size was and whether or not you met it. The target sample sizes should be in your preregistration, and are also available in

the power analysis on your OSF project. By “analytic sample size”, we are referring to the number of observations that were actually included in your analysis after exclusions and handling of missing data, rather than the total number of observations available in your dataset. If you are conducting two stages of data collection or reporting both a No-Overlap dataset and a Hybrid dataset, you will include both results in a single report. Replication Result (stage 1): For stage 1 data collection (analytic N = 60), we found no significant interaction….at stage 1, the replication was unsuccessful according to SCORE criteria. Replication Result (stage 2): At stage 2 (with 40 additional participants, analytic N = 100), the interaction was still not significant...in the final sample, the replication was unsuccessful according to SCORE criteria. If your effect size can be directional, e.g., Cohen’s d, please include a statement like this to help our team and our stats consultants ensure that we are interpreting your results correctly compared to the original study: The claim selected for the original study yielded a [positive/negative] [effect size] in the focal test, corresponding to [description of effect]. The replication yielded a [positive/negative] [effect size] in the focal test, corresponding to an effect in [the same/a different] pattern compared to the original study. Deviations from preregistration. Deviating from your expected plan in some way is common. It’s always better to disclose any differences that will help your readers understand what changed and why. Include brief descriptions of what changed and when from the initial preregistration, and tell us where we can find the relevant details. If you’re not sure whether a change is too small or really matters for your result, err on the side of reporting it! If you are confident that nothing has changed, please state explicitly in this section that there were no deviations from the preregistration. For existing data replications, please disclose whether or not any changes were made to the script(s)/syntax you preregistered. If there were none, or they were minor (e.g., modifying a variable name, changing a file directory, etc.), please include a statement that makes it clear that no substantive changes were made.
